exercises.title: Count the queries — N+1 problem
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Total DB queries: 11
   1 query: SELECT * FROM products WHERE category='Electronics' LIMIT 10
   10 queries: SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = ? (one per product)
   Total: 1 + 10 = 11 queries

2. Query log:
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM products WHERE category='Electronics' LIMIT 10
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P-001'
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P-002'
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P-003'
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P-004'
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P-005'
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P-006'
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P-007'
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P-008'
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P-009'
   [SQL] SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P-010'

3. N+1 name explanation:
   N = 10 (number of products returned)
   +1 = 1 initial query for products
   Total = N+1 = 11 queries
   As N grows: 100 products = 101 queries, 1000 products = 1001 queries
   The problem scales linearly with result size

4. Total review fetch time:
   10 queries × 5ms each = 50ms for reviews
   Plus 5ms for products query = 55ms total
   With DataLoader (batched): 1 query × 5ms = 5ms for ALL reviews
   DataLoader speedup: 10× faster (50ms → 5ms)

hints: N+1: 1 parent query + N child queries (one per parent)
10 products → 10 review queries (each @SchemaMapping call = 1 query)
DataLoader batches all 10 IDs into one IN query

exercises.title: Complete the @BatchMapping resolver
exercises.instructions: Fill in the @BatchMapping that solves the N+1 for product reviews.
hints: @BatchMapping: receives List<ParentType>, returns Map<ParentType, FieldType>
ONE query replaces N queries: findByProductIdIn(ids)
Collectors.toList(): terminal operation collecting stream elements

exercises.title: Demonstrate N+1 with a counter
exercises.instructions:
Write a simulation showing N+1 vs batch:
1. NaiveReviewLoader: simulates 1 query per productId (increments queryCount)
2. BatchReviewLoader: simulates 1 query for all IDs (queryCount=1 always)
3. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): naive=N+1 queries, batch=1 query
hints: queryCount per call: naive increments N times, batch increments once
AtomicInteger: thread-safe counter (GraphQL resolvers can run concurrently)
The difference: 10 queries vs 1 query — 10× improvement

exercises.title: @BatchMapping for Razorpay payments with merchants
exercises.instructions:
Write @BatchMapping for payment→merchant relationship:
1. PaymentResolver with @BatchMapping: Map<Payment, Merchant>
2. MerchantRepository: findByIdIn(List<String>)
3. GroupBy: payment.merchantId → Merchant
4. @SpringBootTest: 5 payments → 1 query returns all merchants
hints: distinct(): avoid duplicate merchant IDs in batch query
Map<String, Merchant> byId: O(1) lookup per payment
5 payments, 3 unique merchants → 1 batch query (not 5)

exercises.title: DataLoader with caching within request
exercises.instructions:
Demonstrate DataLoader caching:
1. CachingDataLoader: tracks cache hits and misses
2. Same productId requested twice → only 1 DB hit (second is cache hit)
3. Different productId → cache miss (DB hit)
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): first request=miss, same=hit, different=miss
hints: DataLoader cache: per-request (cleared after each GraphQL request)
Same key twice in one request: second call is free (cache hit)
Different keys: each is a cache miss (but batched into one DB query)

exercises.title: Flipkart product → reviews @BatchMapping
exercises.instructions:
Build Flipkart GraphQL resolver with @BatchMapping:
1. ProductController: @QueryMapping products() returns 5 products
2. @BatchMapping reviews(List<Product>): returns Map<Product, List<Review>>
3. ReviewRepository: findByProductIdIn(ids) — 1 query
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): batch called once, all products have reviews
hints: @BatchMapping: Spring for GraphQL collects all parent instances, calls method once
findByProductIdIn: JPA derived query — generates SELECT...WHERE product_id IN (?)
filter(r -> r.productId().equals(p.id())): group reviews back to each product

exercises.title: Nested @BatchMapping — 3 levels deep
exercises.instructions:
Zerodha: Order → Trades → Instrument (3-level nesting).
1. @QueryMapping orders() returns 3 orders
2. @BatchMapping trades(List<Order>): Map<Order, List<Trade>>
3. @BatchMapping instrument(List<Trade>): Map<Trade, Instrument>
4. @SpringBootTest: count total DB queries (should be 3: orders + trades + instruments, not N+1)
hints: 3-level depth: orders→trades→instruments. Each @BatchMapping = 1 query per level
Total = 3 queries (not N+1 at each level)
distinct() on instrument IDs: avoid duplicates if multiple trades share an instrument

exercises.title: DataLoader with Mono (reactive)
exercises.instructions:
Write a reactive DataLoader using Mono and CompletableFuture:
1. DataLoaderFactory: creates DataLoader for reviews
2. Batch function: loads all IDs at once, returns CompletableFuture<List<List<Review>>>
3. Each position in output corresponds to same position in input
4. @SpringBootTest: DataLoader registered, batch called once
hints: BatchLoader<K,V>: K=key type, V=value type; returns CompletableFuture<List<V>>
Order matters: output list position must match input list position
loader.dispatch(): triggers the batch call (Spring for GraphQL does this automatically)

exercises.title: Query complexity limiting
exercises.instructions:
Prevent expensive nested queries by limiting complexity:
1. QueryComplexityAnalyzer: calculates field depth and total complexity
2. Assign weights: root field=1, each nesting=2x, list field=multiply by count
3. Reject queries above MAX_COMPLEXITY=100
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): simple passes, deep nesting rejected, high multiplier rejected
hints: Query complexity: prevents malicious deeply-nested queries from hammering DB
2^depth: cost doubles at each nesting level
GraphQL Java library: InstrumentationContext can enforce complexity limits

exercises.title: @SchemaMapping vs @BatchMapping comparison
exercises.expected_solution:
1. DB queries per implementation:
   Impl A (@SchemaMapping): 1 + 5 = 6 queries
     1 query: SELECT * FROM merchants LIMIT 5
     5 queries: SELECT * FROM payments WHERE merchant_id = ? (one per merchant)
   Impl B (@BatchMapping): 1 + 1 = 2 queries
     1 query: SELECT * FROM merchants LIMIT 5
     1 query: SELECT * FROM payments WHERE merchant_id IN ('M1','M2','M3','M4','M5')

2. Query complexity difference:
   If limit=5:  A=6 queries, B=2 queries (3× difference)
   If limit=50: A=51 queries, B=2 queries (25× difference)
   If limit=100: A=101 queries, B=2 queries (50× difference)
   @BatchMapping scales O(1) with parent count; @SchemaMapping scales O(N)

3. Production recommendation:
   @BatchMapping — always for list fields on list parents
   Especially when: merchants are frequently listed with payments
   Razorpay dashboard: showing 50 merchants with their payment counts
   Without @BatchMapping: 51 queries, >500ms latency
   With @BatchMapping: 2 queries, <50ms latency

4. When @SchemaMapping is acceptable:
   Single object (not list): query { merchant(id: 'M1') { payments {...} } }
     Only 1 merchant → 1 payment query → 2 total (same as batch)
   Very rarely accessed: if the 'payments' field is selected <1% of time
   Simple prototype: when building quickly before optimization
   Rule: use @BatchMapping by default for any field on a list type

hints: @BatchMapping: O(1) DB queries regardless of parent count
@SchemaMapping on list parent: O(N) queries (N+1 problem)
Single parent query: @SchemaMapping OK (no N+1 possible)

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay payments + merchants GraphQL
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay GraphQL with @BatchMapping:
1. Schema: Query.payments → [Payment], Payment.merchant → Merchant
2. @QueryMapping payments() → 5 payments
3. @BatchMapping merchant(List<Payment>) → Map<Payment, Merchant>
4. Track query count: must be 2 total (payments + batch merchants)
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): 5 payments, 1 batch merchant query, all merchants found
hints: distinct() in batch: MRC-001 appears twice (PAY-1, PAY-3) → only 1 DB lookup
Map<String, Merchant> byId: O(1) lookup when grouping
assertNotEquals('?', m.id()): verify all payments have a real merchant (not 'Unknown')

exercises.title: DataLoader performance measurement
exercises.instructions:
Build a performance comparator:
1. Run N+1 simulation (N=50 products, each 1 query)
2. Run batch simulation (50 products, 1 query)
3. Measure query count and simulated latency (5ms per query)
4. Assert batch is >10× more efficient
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): naive=51 queries, batch=2 queries, speedup>10x
hints: 51 queries × 5ms = 255ms (naive)
2 queries × 5ms = 10ms (batch)
255/10 = 25.5× speedup — significant for production

exercises.title: Complete Flipkart product catalog GraphQL
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Flipkart GraphQL with 3-level @BatchMapping:
1. @QueryMapping products(category) → List<Product>
2. @BatchMapping seller(List<Product>) → Map<Product, Seller>
3. @BatchMapping reviews(List<Product>) → Map<Product, List<Review>>
4. Track total queries: must be exactly 3 (products + sellers + reviews)
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): products, sellers batched, reviews batched, total=3 queries
hints: Two @BatchMapping methods: seller and reviews — both called once each
distinct() for seller IDs: S1 appears twice (FP1, FP4) → 1 DB lookup
Total: 3 queries (products + sellers + reviews) regardless of N

exercises.title: DataLoader with error handling
exercises.instructions:
Handle partial failures in batch loading:
1. SomeIdsFailBatchLoader: simulates some IDs failing (even IDs throw)
2. BatchResult: success value OR error per key
3. Partial success: return what succeeded, mark failures clearly
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): all succeed, partial failure handled, all fail handled
hints: Partial failure: some items in batch fail, return error for those, success for others
LoadResult: discriminated union (either value or error)
DataLoader Java: can use Try<T> or null + error list for partial failures

exercises.title: Complete M27 T3 DataLoader synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M27 T3 synthesis:
1. 3-level @BatchMapping: orders → payments → merchants
2. Track queries: must be exactly 3
3. Deduplication: same merchant across payments → 1 lookup
4. PerformanceReport: compare naive vs batch query counts
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): 3 orders, payments batched, merchants batched, 3 total queries
hints: 3 levels → 3 queries (1 per level) regardless of parent count
distinct() for merchants: MRC-1 and MRC-2 appear many times → 2 DB lookups
Naive: 1+3+6=10+ queries; batch: 1+1+1=3 queries
